narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a full-time study plan and a low family income background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this law school admission rejection—it's a tough moment, and we're here to help you make sense of it in plain terms. The admission process relies on a predictive model that estimates your chances of passing the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds. For you specifically, the model calculated a 95% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is why your application was denied—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your LSAT score and your Year 3 school grade decile, both of which ranked at the bottom (1st decile) compared to typical students who average around the middle of their classes. Your LSAT score of 18 was especially influential, far below the dataset average of about 37, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar struggles seen in similar past cases. Likewise, your low Year 3 decile stood out as a strong warning sign, even more than your Year 1 decile which also landed in the bottom but had less sway. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, while below the average of 3.21, played only a minor role in tipping the scales toward failure. On a brighter note, your low family income actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, as the model has seen resilient students from similar backgrounds overcome odds. Being full-time, like most applicants (over 90%), offered a small positive boost too. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows weaker spots in standardized testing and later-year grades that the model weighs heavily, putting you in a high-risk group. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted coursework or tutoring, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas—many students in your position have turned things around with focused improvement. You're capable of this, and we're rooting for your next steps.
